1: David’s threefold interrogative — meh-ʿāśîṯî meh-ʿăwōnî ûmeh-ḥaṭṭāʾṯî — “What have I done? What is my iniquity? What is my sin?” The three nouns are a legal formulation of innocence: ʿāwōn (iniquity, moral guilt), ḥēṭʾ (sin, missing the mark), and the general ʿāśāh (wrong done). David presents himself formally as innocent before a court that has condemned him without charge.
2: Jonathan’s response appeals to his own position in Saul’s confidence: lōʾ-yōmar-lî ʾāḇî dāḇār gādôl ʾô dāḇār qāṭōn — “my father does nothing great or small without telling me.” The rhetorical point is that Jonathan should know if Saul planned murder. But the dramatic irony is immediate: Jonathan’s trusted position is precisely why Saul hides his real intent from him (v3).
3: yāḏaʿ yāḏaʿ ʾāḇîḵā — emphatic construction (absolute infinitive + finite verb): “your father knows, he truly knows.” David sees what Jonathan cannot: that Saul conceals his intention precisely because Jonathan’s loyalty to David is known. The phrase wəḵî-penê-yənāṯān yiḵʾāḇ — “lest Jonathan be grieved” — attributes motive to Saul: his concealment is not consideration but calculation.
4: Jonathan’s unconditional offer: kol-ʾăšer-tōmar napšəḵā ʾeʿěśeh-llāḵ — “whatever your soul (nāpāš) asks, I will do for you.” The use of nāpāš (soul/life) in the vow gives it covenantal depth; Jonathan commits the full weight of his life-loyalty to David’s request.
5: David’s plan: the new moon festival (hāḥōdeš). Attendance at the royal feast was obligatory for the inner court — absence would immediately be noticed. David uses his absence as the diagnostic: Saul’s reaction will reveal his intentions. ʾēšēḇ ʾeṣel hammeleḵ leʾĕḵôl — “I should sit with the king at the feast” — the festival meal as a political performance of loyalty.
6: The alibi: a family sacrifice in Bethlehem, David’s hometown. mizbaḥ-yāmîm — “an annual sacrifice” — a regular family sacrificial occasion (cf. Hannah’s annual journey, 1:3). Jonathan is to present it to Saul on David’s behalf. The alibi is plausible and theologically grounded — family sacrifice was a legitimate obligation.
7: ʾim-kōh yōmar ṭôḇ šālôm laʿaḇdəḵā — “if he says ‘good,’ peace to your servant” — the conditional structure maps two outcomes: if Saul accepts the alibi peacefully, David is safe; if he yiḥar-ʾap (becomes angry, lit. “his nose burns”), the intended evil is confirmed. ḥārāh-ʾap is the standard Hebrew idiom for explosive anger, with “nose” as the seat of breath and rage.
8: wəʿāśîṯā ḥesed ʿal-ʿaḇdəḵā — “show covenant loyalty to your servant.” ḥesed (H2617) is the key theological term of this passage — the steadfast, covenantal love that binds the two together. David calls Jonathan to fulfill the specific obligations of their covenant (bərît YHWH, covenanted before YHWH, v8b). If David has done iniquity, Jonathan may kill him himself — the offer of self-judgment as a guarantee of good faith.
9: Jonathan refuses the conditional execution: ḥālîlāh lāḵ — “far from it for you!” (a strong negative expression). His promise: kî ʾim-yāḏaʿ ʾēḏaʿ — “if I know for certain” — again the absolute infinitive for certainty. Jonathan will not withhold the information; the ḥesed he has pledged includes full disclosure when needed.
10: David’s practical question: mî yaggîḏ lî — “who will tell me?” The arrow signal system (vv.20-22) is developed in response to this question. The question reveals the precariousness of the situation: David cannot be at court to receive the information directly; the covenant communication must be covert.
11: lekā wənēṣēʾ haśśāḏeh — “come, let us go out to the field.” The two leave the city for a private space. The field (śāḏeh) is the locus of the covenant renewal; away from court politics, the two friends speak freely. The movement from palace to open field mirrors the shift from institutional to personal covenant.
12: Jonathan’s covenant oath: YHWH ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl kî-ʾeḥqōr ʾeṯ-ʾāḇî — “By YHWH, the God of Israel — when I sound out my father.” ḥāqar (H2713, to search out/probe) is used for careful inquiry, not confrontation. The oath invokes YHWH as guarantor: if the news is good, Jonathan will send it to David; if not, he will send that too.
13: kōh-yōsîp YHWH laYônāṯān wəḵōh yōsîp — the double oath formula (may YHWH add/do more to Jonathan and more still) invokes graduated divine punishment if the oath is broken. The commitment is total: Jonathan promises to actively warn David and help him escape. wahayāh YHWH ʿimmāḵ kaʾăšer hāyāh ʿim-ʾāḇî — “may YHWH be with you as he was with my father” — Jonathan explicitly passes the divine favor from Saul to David.
14: wəʾim-ʿôdennî ḥay wəʿāśîṯā ʿimmî ḥesed YHWH — “if I am still alive, you shall show me the ḥesed of YHWH.” Jonathan’s request turns the ḥesed covenant around: he now asks David for the divine-quality covenant loyalty that David has been asking of him. ḥesed YHWH (the ḥesed of YHWH) is a higher category than human loyalty — it is the kind of love that YHWH himself shows, now required of humans toward one another.
15: wəʾal-takkrēṯ ʾeṯ-ḥasdəḵā mēʿim bêṯî ʿaḏ-ʿôlām — “do not cut off your covenant loyalty from my house forever.” kāraṯ ḥesed (cut off loyalty) is the formal termination of covenant obligation. Jonathan looks beyond his own life to his descendants (bêṯî, my house): the covenant is intergenerational. This provision directly establishes the legal basis for David’s care of Mephibosheth in 2 Sam 9.
16: wayyikrōṯ Yônāṯān ʿim-bêṯ Dāwîḏ — “Jonathan made a covenant with the house of David.” The formal covenant language (kāraṯ bərît) frames this as a dynastic covenant, not merely a personal friendship — it extends to families and successors. ûḇiqqēš YHWH miyyaḏ ʾōyəḇê Dāwîḏ — “and the LORD will require it from the hand of David’s enemies” — YHWH is invoked as the enforcer of the covenant’s anti-enemy clause.
17: wayyôsep Yônāṯān lahaššbîaʿ ʾeṯ-Dāwîḏ bəʾahabāṯô ʾōṯô kî ʾahabaṯ napšô ʾăhēḇô — “Jonathan caused David to swear again because of his love, for he loved him as his own soul.” ʾahabaṯ napšô — “the love of his soul” — echoes the standard formula for deepest personal attachment; cf. 18:1 where the same phrase first appears. The repetition (three mentions of ʾāhab) reinforces that this covenant rests on genuine love, not political calculation.
18: Jonathan outlines the logistical plan: at the new moon feast, David’s seat will be empty (nip̱qaḏtā, you will be missed/your absence will be noticed). The verb pāqaḏ (H6485, to visit/inspect/notice absence) is often used for military musters where an absence is significant; its use here anticipates the formal occasion of the feast.
19: The signal location: the stone Ezel (ʾeḇen hāʾāzel, perhaps “the departing stone” or a proper name). David is to hide there on the third day. The specificity of the geographic marker — a known landmark — anchors the covert communication plan in real terrain.
20: Jonathan will shoot three arrows toward the stone — the number three creates plausible hunting cover. The signal is embedded in a real action that looks innocent to an observer: a young man practicing archery. The code is embedded in the spatial relationship between arrow and target: near = safe; far = flee.
21: ʾim-ʾāmōr ʾōmar lannnaʿar hinnēh haḥiṣṣîm mimmənnaḵā waqqāḥāh — “if I say to the boy, ‘look, the arrows are on this side of you’” — the near-arrow signal means peace. šālôm lāḵ wəʾên dāḇār — “peace to you and there is no thing.” The coded message uses the simplest possible language, interpretable only by those who know the code.
22: wəʾim-kōh ʾōmar laʿelem hinnēh haḥiṣṣîm mimmənnaḵā wāhāʾlāh lēḵ kî šillaḥăḵā YHWH — “but if I say, ‘the arrows are farther out,’ then go, for the LORD has sent you away.” The far-arrow signal encodes a theological frame: David’s departure is not a defeat but divine sending (šālaḥ). YHWH himself is the one dismissing David from court — his flight is obedience to divine direction.
23: wəhaddāḇār ʾăšer dibbarnû ʾănî wāʾattāh hinnēh YHWH bênenû ʿaḏ-ʿôlām — “the matter we have spoken — I and you — the LORD is between us forever.” ʿaḏ-ʿôlām (to eternity) gives the covenant a trans-temporal dimension. YHWH as the standing witness (YHWH bênenû) means the covenant cannot be dissolved by death or changed circumstances.
24: wayyissāṯēr Dāwîḏ baśśāḏeh — David hides in the field while the new moon feast proceeds at court without him. The juxtaposition of David concealed in the field and Saul at the feast underlines the divided reality: the anointed king is in hiding while the rejected king dines in state.
25: The seating arrangement: Saul against the wall (qîr), Abner beside him, Jonathan opposite, David’s place empty. The court seating was fixed and hierarchical; David’s empty place is a visible absence, a gap in the social body that demands explanation.
26: Saul’s first assumption: ritual impurity (ṭāmēʾ hûʾ biltî ṭāhôr). The law required ritual purity for participation in sacred meals; Saul assumes David is unclean rather than absent by choice. The initial charitable interpretation — which Saul himself knows is probably false — keeps the feast proceeding on day one.
27: Day two: maddûaʿ lōʾ-bāʾ ben-Yišay gam-timôl gam-hayyôm ʾel-hallāḥem — “why has Jesse’s son not come to the meal, either yesterday or today?” The deliberate naming of David as “son of Jesse” (ben-Yišay) is a rhetorical reduction — denying David his court title, treating him as a mere commoner.
28: Jonathan delivers the alibi: David asked permission to go to Bethlehem for a family sacrifice. The simplicity and plausibility of the excuse is the point; Jonathan delivers it straightforwardly without elaboration, making it harder to dismiss.
29: yəšallaḥ-nā ʾōṯî — “please let me go.” Jonathan repeats David’s alleged words, emphasizing the family obligation: a brother insisted, a clan sacrifice required attendance. The language of ʾāḥîw ṣiwwāh (my brother commanded me) appeals to kin-obligation — a category Saul himself would recognize as legitimate.
30: ben-naʿăwat hammardût — “son of a perverse, rebellious woman.” This is Saul’s insult to Jonathan — one of the most cutting familial insults in the Hebrew Bible. naʿăwāh (perversity) and mardût (rebellion) attack the mother to shame the son. The eruption reveals what Saul has been suppressing: his certainty that Jonathan’s loyalty to David is treason against the royal house.
31: kî kol-hayyāmîm ʾăšer ben-Yišay ḥay ʿal-hāʾădāmāh lōʾ tikkôn ʾattāh ûmalkûṯəḵā — “as long as Jesse’s son lives, neither you nor your kingdom will stand.” In his rage Saul involuntarily confirms YHWH’s election of David — his own words are the best testimony to David’s coming kingship. lōʾ tikkôn (will not stand/be established) uses the kûn root of divine establishment — the very word YHWH uses for his covenant (2 Sam 7:16).
32: Jonathan’s legal defense: lāmmāh yûmāṯ meh-ʿāśāh — “why should he die? What has he done?” The twice-repeated question is the form of legal defense — demanding specification of the offense before capital punishment. Jonathan makes the procedural argument: no crime has been named.
33: wayyāṭēl šāʾûl ʾeṯ-haḥănîṯ ʿālāyw ləhakkōṯô — “Saul hurled his spear at Jonathan to strike him.” The same violent act aimed at David (18:11; 19:10) is now aimed at his own son. The ḥănîṯ (spear) has become the symbol of Saul’s disorder; its deployment against Jonathan confirms that Saul is past reason.
34: wayyaqom Yônāṯān mēʿim haššulḥān baḥărî-ʾap — “Jonathan rose from the table in fierce anger.” ḥărî-ʾap (burning of nose = fierce anger) is the same idiom used of YHWH’s wrath. Jonathan is angry and grieved (kî huḵlam Dāwîḏ mēʾēt ʾāḇîw) — the grief is for David’s public shaming, not for his own near-death. The covenant loyalty continues to define Jonathan’s emotional priority.
35: Jonathan goes to the field at the appointed time. naʿar qāṭōn (a small boy/young servant) is the instrument of the covert communication — the boy knows only the literal action, not its meaning (v39). The use of an innocent intermediary protects both parties.
36: Jonathan shoots the arrow that “flew past the boy” — the far-arrow signal. wayyôreʾ haḥēṣ lahaʿăḇîrô — “he shot the arrow to make it pass beyond.” The deliberate overshoot encodes the warning in a visible physical act that the boy carries out without understanding its significance.
37: hēn haḥēṣ mimməḵā wāhāʾlāh — “the arrow is beyond you, isn’t it?” The far-arrow is confirmed. The signal is deployed; the danger is real; David must go. Jonathan’s shouted words to the boy are simultaneously a message to David — the public shout serves a double function.
38: maher ḥûšāh ʾal-tāʿmōḏ — “hurry, make haste, don’t stop.” The urgency escalates through three imperatives. The boy gathers the arrows and returns to the city — his errand complete, the drama continuing without his knowledge.
39: wəhannnaʿar lōʾ-yāḏaʿ məʾûmāh — “the boy knew nothing.” This detail closes the frame of the covert communication. The narrator emphasizes the boy’s innocence specifically to underline the technical brilliance of the signal: it was invisible as a message to everyone except its two intended recipients.
40: Jonathan gives his weapons to the boy and sends him away — creating the private space for the farewell. The handing off of weapons (kēlāyw) is significant: Jonathan has already given David his robe, armor, sword, bow, and belt in 18:4; now he removes the remaining implements of court identity.
41: wayyippōl ʾappayim ʾarṣāh wayyištaḥû šālōš pəʿāmîm — “he fell with his face to the ground and bowed three times.” The triple prostration is a royal greeting — David addresses Jonathan with formal court honor even in the wilderness. wayyiḇkû ʾîš ʾeṯ-rēʿēhû — “they wept, each with his friend.” The mutual weeping mirrors the earlier ḥesed language: the covenant is felt as grief at separation.
42: lēḵ ləšālôm ʾăšer nišbaʿnû šənênû ʾănaḥnû bəšēm YHWH — “go in peace, for we have both sworn in the name of YHWH.” The final word of the farewell grounds the covenant in YHWH’s name. bên yādî ûḇên yāḏəḵā ûḇên zarʿî ûḇên zarʿăḵā ʿaḏ-ʿôlām — “between me and you, between my seed and your seed, forever.” The covenant extends to descendants; Jonathan speaks the terms of what will become the Mephibosheth arrangement.
